subject: Company Law
module_title: Introduction to Company Law and Incorporation
lesson_title: LTD vs DAC: Company Types under CA 2014

questions.question: Under the CA 2014, a private limited company (LTD) has how many constitutional documents?

options.A: Two — a memorandum and articles of association
options.B: One — a single document called the constitution
options.C: Three — a memorandum, articles, and a shareholders agreement
options.D: None — the CA 2014 dispenses with constitutional documents for LTDs

questions.explanation: An LTD under the CA 2014 has a single constitutional document called the constitution, replacing the old dual-document regime of memorandum and articles of association.

questions.question: Which company type under the CA 2014 retains an objects clause in its constitution?

options.C: Company limited by guarantee (CLG) without share capital

questions.explanation: The designated activity company (DAC) retains an objects clause, restricting its capacity to the activities specified. This contrasts with an LTD, which has unlimited capacity under section 38 CA 2014.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of directors required for an LTD that does not have a sole director?
questions.explanation: An LTD must have at least two directors (section 128 CA 2014), unless it operates as a sole director company under the single-director regime — in which case it must have one director and a separate secretary.

questions.question: A sole-director LTD must have:

options.A: A separate company secretary who is not also the sole director
options.C: At least two shareholders in addition to the director

questions.explanation: Where an LTD operates with a sole director, section 129 CA 2014 requires that the company has a separate company secretary who is not the same person as the sole director.

questions.question: Under section 38 CA 2014, an LTD has the capacity of:

options.A: A company limited to its stated objects
options.C: A public company, subject to stock exchange rules
options.D: A partnership with joint and several liability

questions.explanation: Section 38 CA 2014 gives an LTD the full legal capacity of a natural person, free from any objects restriction. This is a fundamental distinction from the DAC, which is bound by its stated objects.

questions.question: A DAC must have how many constitutional documents?

options.B: Two — a memorandum and articles of association
options.C: Three — a memorandum, articles, and an objects schedule
options.D: None — it is governed solely by the CA 2014 default rules

questions.explanation: A DAC retains the two-document structure: a memorandum of association (containing the objects clause) and articles of association. This distinguishes it from the LTD which operates with a single constitutional document.

questions.question: Is a private limited company (LTD) required to hold an Annual General Meeting (AGM)?

options.A: Yes — all companies must hold an AGM annually
options.B: No — an LTD is not required to hold an AGM unless the constitution or a member requires it
options.C: Yes — but only if the LTD has more than 50 members
options.D: No — an LTD is exempt from all meeting requirements

questions.explanation: Under section 175 CA 2014, a private limited company (LTD) is not required to hold an AGM unless its constitution so requires or a member serves written notice requesting one. This exemption does not apply to DACs, PLCs, or CLGs.

questions.question: Which company type must hold an AGM within 9 months of its financial year end?

options.D: Only PLCs listed on the Irish Stock Exchange

questions.explanation: Under section 176(1) CA 2014, a DAC, PLC, and CLG must hold an AGM within 9 months of the end of the relevant financial year. LTDs are generally exempt from this obligation.

questions.question: The maximum number of members an LTD may have is:
questions.explanation: Section 17 CA 2014 limits an LTD to a maximum of 149 members (exclusive of employee members), which is a substantial increase from the old 50-member limit.

questions.question: If a company acts outside its objects clause, the transaction is said to be:

options.B: Voidable at the option of the other party
options.C: Ultra vires — though the effect under CA 2014 is modified
options.D: Of no legal consequence, as objects clauses are never enforceable

questions.explanation: Acting outside the objects clause is ultra vires. However, under CA 2014 and the EU First Directive (implemented by section 40 CA 2014), a third party dealing in good faith can enforce a transaction even if it is outside the company's objects, limiting the practical impact of ultra vires for DACs.
